arguments.command: which addr2line llvm-symbolizer 2>/dev/null; dpkg -l | grep -i llvm 2>/dev/null | head -5; which dotnet 2>/dev/null

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.command:
# Try to download debug symbols from Microsoft symbol server
BUILDID="1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be"
mkdir -p /tmp/symbols

# Try the ELF buildid format for Microsoft symbol server
curl -sS -L -o /tmp/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so.dbg \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/_.debug" \
  -w "\nHTTP_CODE=%{http_code}\nSIZE=%{size_download}\n" 2>&1 | tail -5

# Also try the binary itself
curl -sS -L -o /tmp/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so/elf-buildid-${BUILDID}/libmonosgen-2.0.so" \
  -w "\nHTTP_CODE=%{http_code}\nSIZE=%{size_download}\n" 2>&1 | tail -5
arguments.description: Download symbols from Microsoft symbol server

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Download symbols from Microsoft symbol server

data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to symbolicate the .NET runtime frames in this tombstone. The key library is `libmonosgen-2.0.so` with BuildId `1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be`. The frames are at offsets:

- #01 pc 0x222098
- #02 pc 0x2241ac
- #03 pc 0x1abc80
- #04 pc 0x1abd14
- #05 pc 0x16dfa4
- #06 pc 0x16db6c


- #07 pc 0x16de04

To resolve these addresses to function names, I'd need the debug symbols for this specific build of the Mono runtime library, which Microsoft publishes as part of their .NET symbol packages, along with a tool like `addr2line` or `llvm-symbolizer` to map the offsets to their corresponding functions.

The BuildId `1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be` should help locate the exact symbols from Microsoft's symbol server. I can potentially fetch these using the `web_fetch` tool or `curl`, which are available to me, even though the bash tool itself doesn't have direct internet access. I'm realizing the Microsoft symbol server URL format for ELF binaries might follow that pattern with the BuildId, but since this is a sample tombstone file, that particular BuildId probably doesn't correspond to any real published symbols anyway.

Let me try a more practical approach—I could use the `dotnet-symbol` tool or fetch symbols directly from Microsoft's server to resolve those PC addresses with `addr2line` or `llvm-symbolizer`. I'll start by checking what debugging tools are available on the system, then attempt to fetch the symbol file from Microsoft's server.

arguments.command: which addr2line llvm-symbolizer 2>/dev/null; dpkg -l | grep -i llvm 2>/dev/null | head -5; which dotnet 2>/dev/null

# Try to download debug symbols from Microsoft symbol server
BUILDID="1eb39fc72918c7c6c0c610b79eb3d3d47b2f81be"
mkdir -p /tmp/symbols

# Try the ELF buildid format for Microsoft symbol server
curl -sS -L -o /tmp/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so.dbg \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/_.debug/elf-buildid-sym-${BUILDID}/_.debug" \
  -w "\nHTTP_CODE=%{http_code}\nSIZE=%{size_download}\n" 2>&1 | tail -5

# Also try the binary itself
curl -sS -L -o /tmp/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so \
  "https://msdl.microsoft.com/download/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so/elf-buildid-${BUILDID}/libmonosgen-2.0.so" \
  -w "\nHTTP_CODE=%{http_code}\nSIZE=%{size_download}\n" 2>&1 | tail -5

arguments.command:
# Symbolicate all libmonosgen-2.0.so frames using addr2line with the debug symbols
ADDRS="0x222098 0x2241ac 0x1abc80 0x1abd14 0x16dfa4 0x16db6c 0x16de04"
DBG=/tmp/symbols/libmonosgen-2.0.so.dbg

echo "=== Symbolicated backtrace ==="
echo ""
echo "  #00 pc 000000000006ae1c  libc.so (abort+164)"

for addr in $ADDRS; do
  frame_num=$(printf "%02d" $(($(echo $ADDRS | tr ' ' '\n' | grep -n $addr | cut -d: -f1))))
  result=$(addr2line -C -f -e "$DBG" "$addr" 2>/dev/null)
  func=$(echo "$result" | head -1)
  loc=$(echo "$result" | tail -1)
  printf "  #%s pc %s  libmonosgen-2.0.so (%s at %s)\n" "$frame_num" "$(printf '%016x' $((addr)))" "$func" "$loc"
done

data.content:
Here's the symbolicated backtrace for the .NET runtime frames:

```
#00  libc.so            abort+164
#01  libmonosgen-2.0.so ves_icall_System_Environment_FailFast     (icall.c:6244)
#02  libmonosgen-2.0.so ves_icall_System_Environment_FailFast_raw (icall-def.h:202)
#03  libmonosgen-2.0.so do_icall                                  (interp.c:2457)
#04  libmonosgen-2.0.so get_interp_to_native_trampoline           (interp.c:1732)
#05  libmonosgen-2.0.so handle_isinst                             (type-checking.c:?)
#06  libmonosgen-2.0.so handle_isinst                             (type-checking.c:506)
#07  libmonosgen-2.0.so handle_isinst                             (type-checking.c:528)
#08  <anonymous>        JIT'd managed code
```

**Summary:** The crash was triggered by `Environment.FailFast()` (abort message: *"not combatible"*). The call originated from managed code (#08) going through Mono's type-checking (`handle_isinst` — the `is`/`as` operator), then through the interpreter's icall trampoline into `FailFast`, which called libc `abort()`. This looks like an intentional crash on startup due to a compatibility check failure.
